verdict: A GP landing here would sense they've found the right specialist — but leave without enquiring, because the site never makes them feel understood, hides the price, and gives no easy way to book.
scope_note: This audit looks at strategy, messaging, and content — the things that determine whether a visitor becomes an enquiry. Design is a whole separate conversation, and one that's much more meaningful once we understand your clients, your goals, and what you're trying to achieve. That's a call worth having.

fixes.title: A doctor who's ready to book can't — so they don't
fixes.detail: A GP finishes reading your site at 9pm between patients. They're interested. They hit Contact, fill in the form, and wait. By the time you reply, they've either forgotten or found someone else who had a booking link. The form creates a gap between 'I'm interested' and 'I've booked' — and that gap is where most enquiries disappear.
fixes.action: Add a Calendly link directly on the contact page and in the main call to action so someone can book a 20-minute call in 30 seconds without waiting for a reply.

fixes.title: Silence on price sends doctors straight to Google
fixes.detail: A doctor landing here for the first time — probably already wary of accountancy fees — will look for a price before doing anything else. When there isn't one, they don't ask. They assume expensive and move on to someone willing to give them a ballpark. You don't need a price list, just enough for the right people to self-select.
fixes.action: Add one short paragraph explaining how you charge — whether that's a monthly retainer, an annual fee, or a 'typically starts at' figure — so the right people stay and the wrong ones don't waste your time.

fixes.title: The site talks about you when it should be talking to them
fixes.detail: Almost every section of the homepage opens with 'we', 'our', or 'at DrAccountant' — the business is the main character of its own website. A GP reading this feels like they're being pitched to rather than understood, and people don't hire businesses that pitch at them; they hire ones that seem to already get their situation.
fixes.action: Go section by section and flip the opening sentence of each so it starts with 'you' — 'You get an accountant who knows what an NHS pension looks like from the inside' lands harder than 'We specialise in NHS pension advice'.

fixes.title: Three blog posts and a 2019 date signal the site was set up and never touched
fixes.detail: A prospect who clicks your blog to check your expertise finds a post dated March 2019 with a web address ending in random letters — the platform default from the day the site launched. It's a small thing, but to a doctor deciding who to trust with their finances, it signals that no one has looked at this in years, which is the opposite of the reassurance they need before picking up the phone.
fixes.action: Publish one real article written by Dr. Othman on a GP tax issue that doctors actually search for (and give it a proper web address), then update the case study addresses from /case-study-2 to something matching the title.

findings.note: The site is unambiguously positioned for healthcare professionals only, and the GP-founded angle is prominent. This survives the name-removal test — strip the logo and it still couldn't belong to a general accountancy firm.

Founded by a practising GP who personally navigated NHS pension and tax complexity is a differentiator that can't be faked or easily copied. It's specific, credible, and immediately meaningful to a doctor.

findings.check: Site puts the client first, not the business
findings.note: The homepage opens with a doctor-focused metaphor, which is a good start, but shifts to 'we specialize', 'we offer', 'we understand' within two paragraphs — the business becomes the main character. When visitors read about you instead of recognising their own situation, they disengage; people only stay engaged with content that feels like it's about them.
findings.rec: Rewrite each homepage section so the first sentence is about the doctor's situation, not the business's capabilities.

findings.check: The core problem is named clearly
findings.note: NHS pension complexity and multiple income streams are mentioned, but as features of the service rather than the real source of stress a doctor carries. Without naming the core problem clearly there's no tension — and without tension there's no reason for a visitor to feel they need to do something about it.
findings.rec: Name the core problem directly — 'the NHS pension is the most complex financial decision a doctor makes, and most get it wrong' — and put it front and centre above the fold.

findings.note: Blog posts are dated December 2024 — with one showing a 2019 placeholder date that was never updated — and the content reads like it was written at launch and never revisited. A doctor who notices this will wonder whether the business is still actively engaged with its clients, or just coasting.
findings.rec: Replace agency-authored content with at least one article and one case study written by the founders.

findings.note: The services page names Tax Planning and Bookkeeping & Payroll but gives no detail on what's included, how the engagement works, or what it costs. If a doctor has to contact you just to understand what they'd be buying, most won't bother — they'll go to a competitor who answers that question upfront.
findings.rec: Add a brief scope note under each service explaining what's included and how delivery works, plus a pricing anchor.

findings.check: Main call to action and a lower-commitment option both present
findings.note: Every CTA on the site pushes straight to booking a consultation, with nothing for a doctor who's interested but not ready to commit yet. Most visitors won't book on a first visit — without a lower-stakes option like a guide or newsletter, those warm leads leave with no way to bring them back.
findings.rec: Add one transitional option — a downloadable resource on NHS pension decisions, or a newsletter signup — so there's a path for warm visitors who aren't ready to book yet.

findings.check: Genuine understanding of the client's situation shown
findings.note: The About page origin story is genuine — Dr. Othman took a sabbatical to learn finance after experiencing the exact stress the target client feels. That's real empathy, not a marketing claim.
Dr. Othman holds MSc, PhD, and MRCGP credentials alongside firsthand GP experience; Lucas holds MAAT with 15 years in healthcare accounting. Both are visible and credible.

findings.check: Opening headline connects to the client's desire or situation
findings.note: The headline 'Caring for your Accounts, Like you care for Patients' is clever and memorable — you understand what they do within seconds. But it names the service category rather than the deeper desire a doctor actually has: financial security, the freedom to focus on patients, or knowing retirement is sorted — the things that make getting the finances right feel worth doing.
findings.rec: Test a headline that names what the doctor actually wants from life, not what DrAccountant does: something like 'Focus on patients. We'll make sure the money works.' or 'The financial clarity that lets you practice medicine without the weight of the numbers.'

findings.check: The emotional frustration behind the practical problem is addressed
findings.note: The real emotional driver — the stress of managing finances while running an already demanding medical career — is buried on the About page rather than leading the homepage. People act on emotional problems, not logical ones; moving this to where the decision is actually made changes the entire conversion dynamic.
findings.rec: Move one sentence about the emotional reality of being a doctor managing finances into the opening section of the homepage.

findings.note: The 'How it works' section has three steps, but step 2 is 'Let Us Work Our Magic' — which tells a cautious doctor nothing about what actually happens to their money, their data, or their time. A vague process feels like a risk; a clear one feels like a decision you can make confidently.
findings.rec: Replace the vague middle step with something concrete: 'We review your income streams, pension position, and tax exposure, then build a plan tailored to your career stage.'

findings.note: Peace of mind, clarity, and confidence appear throughout, but they're abstract — every accountancy firm says something similar. Specific outcomes ('know exactly what your NHS pension will pay out before you make a career decision that affects it') create genuine desire in a way that vague promises never can.
findings.rec: Add one specific outcome sentence — 'Know exactly what your NHS pension will pay out before you make a single career decision that affects it.'

findings.check: The bigger picture — what's really at stake — comes through
findings.note: There's no sense of the bigger picture — no acknowledgement that financial mistakes cost doctors years of retirement security, or that getting this right is what enables the career and life they've worked two decades to build. Without that framing, hiring an accountant feels like a chore; with it, it feels like one of the most important decisions they'll make.
findings.rec: Add one sentence about what's really at stake: getting NHS pension decisions wrong isn't a financial inconvenience, it's a retirement problem that takes decades to play out.

findings.check: There's a reason to act now, not later
findings.note: Nothing on the site suggests what a doctor is silently losing by not having this sorted — no mention of overpaying tax, pension penalties, or missed allowances that can't be recovered after the fact. Without a reason to act now rather than later, most visitors will bookmark the site and never come back.
findings.rec: Add one line to the opening section about what's silently at risk: 'Most doctors overpay tax by thousands every year simply because their accountant doesn't know how NHS income works.'

findings.check: Clear within 5 seconds what you do and who for
findings.note: Within 5 seconds it's clear: specialist accountancy for doctors, run by a GP and an accountant, book a free consultation. The headline and tagline work together for fast comprehension — though whether it makes the right doctor feel found is a separate question, covered in Messaging.

findings.check: Services explain outcomes, not just names
findings.note: Tax Planning gets partway there — 'minimizes tax liabilities' is a result — but it stops short of something a busy doctor can actually feel. People buy outcomes, not service names; 'knowing exactly what you'll owe before HMRC asks' lands differently than 'we minimise your tax liabilities.'
findings.rec: Add one outcome sentence per service that describes what life looks like after the problem is solved, not just what the service does.

findings.note: The Squarespace layout helps with spacing, but the 'Why us' section has six blocks of paragraph text in a row — a doctor scanning on their phone between patients will give up before reaching the end. Content that doesn't get read doesn't do any work.
findings.rec: Reduce each 'Why us' block to two lines max, or convert the section to a simple icon-plus-one-line format.

findings.check: Copy leads with client, not business
findings.note: A count of the homepage shows 'we', 'our', and 'at DrAccountant' opening most major sections — the services page alone opens with 'Our healthcare accountants are experts.' When the business is the subject of almost every sentence, a visitor reads it as a sales pitch rather than a service built around them.
findings.rec: Audit every section heading and opening line: if it starts with 'we' or 'our', rewrite it so the doctor is the subject.

findings.note: Section headings across the site — 'Tailored Accountancy Solutions', 'Why us', 'How it works' — describe what's coming rather than giving a reason to read it. A heading that hints at something useful pulls the reader forward; a label just marks territory.
findings.rec: Rewrite at least the three most-read section headings so they pull the visitor forward: 'Why most doctors overpay — and how to stop' beats 'Why us' every time.

findings.note: 'Tailored' appears repeatedly throughout the site — in headlines, service descriptions, and the 'why us' section — alongside 'comprehensive', 'seamless', and 'dedicated'. Every accountancy website in the country uses these words, which means they give a visitor no reason to choose this one over any other.
findings.rec: Do a find-and-replace on 'tailored', 'comprehensive', and 'personalized' — every instance should be replaced with a specific claim or deleted entirely.

findings.note: There is no mention of cost, fees, or pricing structure anywhere — not a 'from' figure, not even an explanation of how you charge. A doctor who can't get a rough sense of the investment will either assume the worst and leave, or contact you just to ask the price — which is the most avoidable friction in the whole enquiry process.
findings.rec: Add a pricing section or a short paragraph on the services page explaining how you charge — monthly retainer, annual fee, or a starting-from figure — so the right people self-qualify.

findings.check: About page makes the owner human and connects to the client's problem
findings.note: Real photos of both founders, a genuine origin story (Dr. Othman took a sabbatical to learn finance after experiencing the exact stress the target client feels), and clear credentials. The story earns trust before a service is mentioned.
Dr. Othman's MSc, PhD, and MRCGP are prominent; Lucas's MAAT and 15 years of experience are clearly stated. These are external, verifiable markers of competence.

Three testimonials are named with role and location — Dr. Emily Carter (Sessional GP, Manchester), Dr. James Robinson (GP Partner, London), Dr. Adam Wilson (Plastic Surgeon, Leeds) — which is better than most. Without photos or dates though, a sceptical reader has no way to verify these are real people, which quietly reduces their impact.
findings.rec: Add a photo and approximate date to each testimonial, and tighten the language to sound like something a doctor actually said rather than a polished summary.

findings.note: The 'Saving Over £30,000' case study has a credible result and relevant detail, which is genuinely useful for a doctor evaluating whether to get in touch. The web addresses are still Squarespace defaults — /case-study-2, /case-study-3 — which signals to anyone who notices that the site was set up and left, and subtly undermines the credibility of everything else on the page.
findings.rec: Update the web addresss to match the case study titles and verify the content reflects how the business actually works today.

findings.note: The contact page is a plain form with no testimonial, credential, or reassurance about what happens next. The moment someone decides to get in touch is exactly when doubt is loudest — that's when trust signals do their most important work, and right now there are none at that critical point.
findings.rec: Add one short testimonial or a founder headshot with a brief trust line directly above the contact form.

findings.note: There is no Google Business profile, no Trustpilot, and no review presence on any platform a doctor would check when choosing a specialist. Without third-party proof, everything on the site is self-reported — and most buyers discount self-reported claims heavily when they can't find independent confirmation anywhere.
findings.rec: Set up a Google Business profile and start collecting reviews there — it's free, it shows up in search results, and it's the first place a doctor will look.

findings.note: NHS pension complexity and multiple income streams are mentioned, but only as context for what DrAccountant solves — there's no honest discussion of limitations, who isn't a good fit, or what can go wrong. Businesses that only present the positive picture tend to feel like they're hiding something; honesty about limitations actually builds more trust, not less.
findings.rec: Add a short 'who this is for' and 'who this isn't for' note to the services page so visitors can self-qualify.

findings.note: The site lists doctors, consultants, dentists, and allied health professionals as the audience, which helps a visitor know they're in the right place. But there's no trigger — nothing that says 'if this is your situation, now is the moment to act' — which means interested visitors can leave feeling informed but not compelled.
findings.rec: Add a brief qualifier — 'If you're managing NHS and private income, approaching a pension decision, or have never had an accountant who understood medical finance, this is for you.'

findings.note: No pricing information exists anywhere on the site — see Copywriting for the full finding. For context: when asked what they most want to know before contacting a service business, 'how much does it cost' is the answer most buyers give.
findings.rec: N/A — see Copywriting section.

findings.note: The site doesn't address why DrAccountant over a generalist accountant, a larger firm, or simply staying put with who they have. Most potential clients will already have an accountant — without help making the comparison, a doctor thinking 'mine seems fine' has nothing to challenge that assumption.
findings.rec: Add a short comparison section or FAQ entry: 'What makes you different from my current accountant?'

findings.note: The three blog posts cover surface-level topics and one still shows a 2019 placeholder date that was never updated — none demonstrate the specialist expertise a doctor is actually looking for. A business that teaches what it knows builds trust before any sales conversation starts; a blog that looks untouched does the opposite.
findings.rec: Replace the agency content with one article written by Dr. Othman on a real, common issue — NHS pension annual allowance charges, for example — to demonstrate expertise before a call.

findings.check: One clear next step on every page
findings.note: 'Book your Free Consultation Today' is the single, consistent call to action across every page — one obvious next step everywhere. Giving visitors multiple CTAs at once splits their attention and reduces the chance they act on any of them, so this is exactly right.

Homepage to contact form is a single click, with the CTA button in the nav and repeated throughout every page. Every extra step between interest and action loses a percentage of visitors — this removes almost all of that friction.

The homepage introduces the business before establishing any problem or tension — 'who we are' comes before a visitor has been given a reason to care. A visitor who doesn't feel the problem first won't feel the value of the solution, so leading with the business rather than the client's situation costs you the emotional connection that drives action.
findings.rec: Move the 'why us' points earlier and lead with the client's situation before introducing the business.

findings.note: The contact form requires five fields, a reCAPTCHA, and then a wait for a manual reply before anything is confirmed. That gap between clicking 'get in touch' and having a call actually booked is where most enquiries fall apart — a direct booking link removes it entirely.
findings.rec: Add a Calendly link alongside the form — see Fixes — so instant booking is possible.

findings.check: Navigation serves new visitors, not existing clients
findings.note: The nav has a redundant Home link (the logo already does this) and lists Resources before Contact — which buries the most important action behind content most first-time visitors won't want first. Nav order signals priority; anything that pushes Contact further right reduces the chance it gets clicked.
findings.rec: Remove the Home link and move Contact before Resources in the nav order.

findings.note: The contact page gives no indication of what happens after you submit — how quickly you'll hear back, what the first call looks like, or that there's no obligation attached. Uncertainty at the exact moment of action is one of the most common reasons people don't follow through, even after they've decided they want to.
findings.rec: Add three lines below the form: when you'll hear back, what the first call covers, and that there's no obligation.

findings.check: Content is easy to find and navigate
findings.note: Resources is in the main nav and one click away, which is the right instinct. With only three posts though, discoverability isn't the problem — the lack of content worth finding is.
findings.rec: Once real content exists, add category tags so visitors can find relevant posts quickly.

findings.check: Content hub exists with real, recent articles
findings.note: The Resources page has three posts — two from December 2024 and one with a 2019 placeholder date that was never changed — all with auto-generated web addresses that were never updated. A blog that looks abandoned is worse than no blog: it signals a business that doesn't follow through, which is a damaging message for an accountant to send.
findings.rec: Publish at least one genuine article from Dr. Othman or Lucas with a properly written web address — even one real post immediately changes the credibility of the page.

findings.check: Content covers the topics buyers actually search for
findings.note: The three posts cover generic financial topics that don't demonstrate specialist medical accounting expertise — none tackle the specific questions a doctor types into Google before choosing an accountant. Content that answers real buying questions is what brings qualified visitors to the site in the first place; the current posts won't attract them or convert them.
findings.rec: Plan five posts: 'How much does a specialist medical accountant cost?', 'NHS pension annual allowance — what most doctors get wrong', 'Do I need a specialist or will any accountant do?', a client story, and a how-to guide.

findings.note: There are no FAQs anywhere on the site, which means the questions a doctor has before picking up the phone — cost, process, what makes you different from their current accountant — go completely unanswered. Every unanswered question becomes a reason to hesitate rather than a reason to get in touch.
findings.rec: Add a simple FAQ section to the services or contact page covering cost, process, and who this is for.

findings.note: There is no video anywhere on the site. A 90-second clip of Dr. Othman explaining why he set this business up would do more trust-building in one watch than every paragraph on the page — seeing and hearing a GP say 'I've been through exactly what you're dealing with' is something no written sentence can replicate.
findings.rec: Record a 90-second video of Dr. Othman explaining why he co-founded DrAccountant and embed it on the homepage or About page.

findings.check: Guided path to prepare visitors before they get in touch
findings.note: There's no guided path for a doctor who wants to do their research before committing to a call — no suggested reading, no 'here's what to know before we speak.' A prospect who arrives having already read three pieces of your content is already half-sold; right now, that opportunity doesn't exist.
findings.rec: Create a simple 'Before your consultation' page that links to two or three pieces of content a prospect should read first — it pre-educates leads and shortens the first call significantly.

findings.check: Content helps visitors feel ready before they make contact
findings.note: The three blog posts cover generic topics that don't tackle the specific questions that come up before a first call, like NHS pension annual allowance or how to structure private practice income. When content does this job well, first calls are faster and clients arrive more qualified; right now the site sends none of that groundwork.
findings.rec: N/A — see 'Content hub exists' above; fixing that fixes this.

findings.note: The site loads securely and a padlock appears in the browser — no warnings or errors for visitors. This matters because a security warning before a word is read is enough to make many visitors leave immediately, so getting this right is non-negotiable.

findings.check: Easy to update without a developer
findings.note: Built on Squarespace, which means the founders can update any page, add a blog post, or change a testimonial without calling a developer. Sites that depend on external help to make any change go stale quickly — and a stale site costs enquiries.

findings.check: Easy to find when someone searches for you by name
findings.note: Searching for 'DrAccountant' returns no results for this site — unrelated pages appear instead. Anyone who's been referred or heard the name and tries to look it up won't find it, which means word-of-mouth referrals fall through the gap before they've even seen the site.
findings.rec: Check that the site title and meta description include 'DrAccountant' clearly, and set up a Google Business profile — both help Google connect a name search to the right website.

findings.check: Contact forms and booking tools working
findings.note: The contact form is present and all CTA buttons link correctly to the contact page. However there is no booking tool — a doctor who wants to lock in a time has to wait for a manual response, which adds an entirely avoidable step between deciding to act and actually acting.
findings.rec: Add a Calendly or equivalent booking link so visitors can self-schedule — see Fixes.

findings.check: Web addresses are clear and descriptive
findings.note: Blog posts and case studies use Squarespace's auto-generated web addresses — /blog-post-title-one-fsjre and /case-study-2 — which were never updated when the content was published. These rank for nothing in search results and signal to anyone who notices that the site was built and left.
findings.rec: Update each post and case study URL to match its title — Squarespace lets you edit the web address in page settings without a developer.

positives: GP-founded positioning is a genuine differentiator — it survives the name-removal test and is immediately meaningful to a doctor evaluating accountants.
Dr. Othman's origin story on the About page is real and compelling — it answers 'why should I trust this person?' before a word of sales copy appears.
Real founder photos throughout the site — no stock handshakes, which immediately sets a warmer and more trustworthy tone.
